# nicl

<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

The aim of this package is to build a consistent and tested suite of
code for analysing Intracluster Light within the Nottingham ICL group.
Ultimately, this package – or parts of it – will be shared with
collaborators and made public so that others can reproduce our published
analyses.

## Installation

This software is still in active development, but is starting to be used
within the Nottingham ICL group.

### Basic install

If you only want to use (not develop) the software, you can install it
(and its dependencies) into an environment by cloning this repository,
changing to the root repository folder, and running:

    pip install .

### Development install

To install the software for development, first create a clean Python
(\>= 3.12) environment. Clone this repository and change to the root
repository folder. With your environment activated, install nbdev by
running:

    pip install -U nbdev

Then install nicl and all its dependencies with:

    pip install -e '.[dev]'

The `-e` here performs an ‘editable’ installation, i.e. the files in the
repository are used directly, rather than being copied to a central
location.

If you prefer to use conda, you can alternatively first install
dependencies using:

    conda env create -f environment.yaml

followed by

    pip install -e '.[dev]'

The core code, documentation, tests and applications are written in
Jupyter notebooks. To faciliate this, the development of this software
uses [nbdev](https://nbdev.fast.ai). To understand this system, you
should familiarise yourself with the [introductory
walkthrough](https://nbdev.fast.ai/tutorials/tutorial.html).

To ensure the code committed to the repository is well-formatted and
correctly cleaned and exported, we use some git commit hooks. To enable
these run:

    pre-commit install

Now, whenever you try to commit to this repo, a set of checks will run.

#### Committing changes to git

Start a commit by doing `git add your_new_or_updated_notebooks.ipynb`.
Then run `git commit -m "Your informative log message"`. Typically the
notebook will be reformatted, cleaned and exported, and the checks will
fail. Next, do `git add your_new_or_updated_notebooks.ipynb` again, and
also add any new files that have been created in the `nicl` folder. You
might find `git add -u .` helpful, but be careful not to add any files
you have changed but don’t want to commit at this stage. Then run
`git commit -m "Your informative log message"` again. You will likely
need to go through this routine one more time, then the commit will
succeed. If it doesn’t, there may be a problem with your code, which you
will need to fix before commiting. If you get desperate, you can skip
the checks with `git commit -n`, then let Steven know so he can
investigate.

## Structure

The notebooks are in the [`nbs`
folder](https://github.com/IntraClusterLight/nicl/tree/main/nbs). These
are automatically exported by nbdev to produce the library in the
[`nicl`
folder](https://github.com/IntraClusterLight/nicl/tree/main/nicl). The
notebooks can use code defined in each other by importing the
pip-installed library, i.e. `import nicl`. The `nicl` folder can also
contain additional python code that is not generated from the notebooks;
this currently includes a modified version of ezgal.

The root [`nbs`
folder](https://github.com/IntraClusterLight/nicl/tree/main/nbs)
contains notebooks defining library functionality that should be of
general use in analysing ICL in a variety of datasets. Within that
folder are subfolders containing notebooks defining library
functionality tailored to specific datasets, e.g. HSC and Euclid. Those
folders can also contain notebooks that perform the steps for a
particular analysis, making use of the functionality in the nicl
library.

- Move code into functions (abstracted where obvious, but don’t do too
  much at this time)

### Advanced ICL measurement

Work towards a more advanced approach to measuring ICL is in the [ficl
repository](https://github.com/IntraClusterLight/ficl). The aim is to
eventually make use of this within nicl.

#### ficl and AstroPhot

- Install fork of [AstroPhot](https://github.com/Autostronomy/AstroPhot)
  for development
- Use AstroPhot to fit model galaxies a simulated Euclid cluster
- Try implementing ficl iterative subtraction into AstroPhot

#### Scarlet

- Install fork of
  [Scarlet](https://pmelchior.github.io/scarlet/index.html) for
  development
- Try using Scarlet to decompose ICL and galaxies in a simulated Euclid
  cluster
